details.title: Make PGlite migration path idempotent
edge_cases: Empty migration list: tracking table created (idempotent), loop body never enters — safe
Concurrent calls on :memory: PGlite: single-connection per instance, PRIMARY KEY prevents dupes — safe
Partial migration failure: hash inserted only after ALL SQL statements succeed, so next run retries the entire migration atomically
Hash collision: Drizzle uses SHA-256 hashes; collision probability is negligible
Tracking table name: __drizzle_migrations matches Drizzle's node-postgres built-in schema, ensuring compatibility if backend switches later
details.existing_test_behavior: All 4 existing tests pass unchanged — they call applyMigrations() once and verify tables exist. Tracking table creation is idempotent and invisible to existing assertions.
risks: PGlite query Rows type: PGlite's query<T>() returns { rows: Row<T>[] }. The code accesses result.rows directly. If PGlite's type returns rows differently (e.g., result is an array), the ?? fallback handles it.

TypeScript generics override: client.query<{ hash: string }>() uses a type parameter. If PGlite's query signature doesn't accept generics this way, the type assertion may need adjustment. The codebase already uses this pattern in checkPgHealth.
Migration hash format: Drizzle's readMigrationFiles produces SHA-256 hex strings. Confirmed by inspecting the snapshot.json files which contain hashes like 'd1c2e3f4...' — standard 64-char hex.
$client internal access: existing code already uses (db as any).$client — no new coupling introduced.
validation: bun test packages/opencode/test/storage/migration.pg.test.ts — all 3 tests pass (2 existing + 1 new)
bun typecheck from packages/opencode — no new type errors
Manual: run a script that calls applyMigrations() twice on :memory: PGlite — second call no-ops
